Is this safe to use if I'm on prescriptions?

The handbook is an educational reference designed specifically for people who are on medications. It includes Contraindication Quick-Scan Tables and 'when to avoid' flags for each herb. It is not a substitute for medical advice — it's a tool to support better-informed conversations with your prescriber.
What meds does the interaction guidance cover?

The handbook covers the most common drug classes relevant to anxiety and sleep: gabapentin and related anticonvulsants, benzodiazepines, SSRIs and SNRIs, hormone therapies including HRT patches and pills, and common OTC sleep aids. A 'Common Interaction Tripwires' checklist is also included.
The handbook is a digital download in PDF format, accessible immediately after purchase on any device. The Quick-Scan Sheets and Tracker are printable if you prefer a physical reference.
Email us within 30 days for a full refund. No questions designed to talk you out of it. The 30-day no-guessing guarantee is unconditional.

Bonus #4: Patch/Pill Change Tracker — a simple log to spot patterns after a med or hormone change. Patterns become visible. Guessing becomes optional.

config.title: Why Herb Guides Fail People on Prescriptions
paragraphs: If gabapentin made your anxiety worse — or HRT sent your symptoms in the wrong direction — you already know the frustration of looking for a gentler alternative and hitting a wall.

Most herb guides hand you a list. Ashwagandha. Lemon balm. Valerian. Maybe a dosage range. What they almost never include: which of those herbs amplify sedation when combined with CNS-active medications, which ones conflict with hormone-based therapies, or when you should call your doctor instead of trying something new.

One person put it plainly: 'I'm on several prescription meds and I'm always hesitant to use herbs because I can't find interaction info.' Those aren't edge-case questions. They're the question most herb guides quietly ignore — and the gap that creates real risk.
config.emphasisLine: The problem isn't herbs. The problem is herb guides written as if nobody takes prescriptions.

Soft natural light, calm and organized aesthetic. No faces. Contextual lifestyle scene. config.title: How the Contraindication Quick-Scan Tables Work

bullets.body: A symptom-to-options index points you to conservative starting choices for post-medication or post-HRT anxiety — not every herb ever studied, just the ones with the gentlest profiles for your context.
bullets.title: Start with your situation, not the herb

bullets.body: Every herb entry carries a flag: Green (generally well-tolerated), Yellow (check with prescriber first), Red (avoid with this drug class). You see it in seconds, not after reading three paragraphs.
bullets.title: Run the Contraindication Quick-Scan

bullets.body: Each entry includes a conservative starting range and plain-language notes on when that herb is not appropriate — specific drug classes, health conditions, and timing considerations included.
bullets.title: Check dosage and 'when to avoid' notes

bullets.body: The included Patch/Pill Change Tracker lets you log what you tried, when, and what changed. Patterns become visible. Guessing becomes optional.
bullets.title: Track what happens

callout.leftBody: Organized by plant. Interaction data buried in footnotes or missing entirely. Generic dosage ranges. No tracking tools. Written as if nobody takes prescriptions.
callout.rightBody: Organized by situation. Contraindication Quick-Scan flags built into every entry. Conservative dosage for post-med/HRT context. Tracking tools and clinician conversation pack included.

paragraphs: A published review in the British Journal of Clinical Pharmacology found that herb-drug interactions are underreported and rarely flagged in consumer references. The consequence: people combining sedating herbs with CNS-active medications without knowing the additive risk. That's what happens when herb guides skip the interaction data.

The Honest Herbalist Handbook is built around triage logic — your situation first, the herb second. Every entry carries a Quick-Scan flag so you know in seconds whether an herb is appropriate for your medication context.

config.title: What You Get vs. What Most Herb Guides Provide

config.title: The 30-Day No-Guessing Money-Back Guarantee
config.whyBody: Because we're confident the handbook delivers what it promises: a safety-screened, organized reference built for people already on medications who need real interaction guidance. If it doesn't do that for you, you shouldn't pay for it.
paragraphs: If the Honest Herbalist Handbook isn't clearer, more organized, and more useful for your medication situation than searching forums — email us within 30 days for a full refund. No forms, no hoops, no questions designed to talk you out of it.
We offer this guarantee because the people who need this handbook most have already been burned by incomplete information. You should be able to try it without risk.
config.closingLine: This handbook is an educational reference only — not medical care, diagnosis, or treatment. Always consult your healthcare provider before making changes to your health routine.

items.question: Is this handbook safe to use if I'm on prescription medications?

items.answer: The handbook covers the most common drug classes relevant to anxiety and sleep: gabapentin and related anticonvulsants, benzodiazepines, SSRIs and SNRIs, hormone therapies including HRT patches and pills, and common OTC sleep aids. A 'Common Interaction Tripwires' checklist is also included.
items.question: What medications does the interaction guidance cover?

items.answer: That's exactly who this handbook is written for. The Contraindication Quick-Scan Tables are designed to give you a safety screen before you try anything — not a list of things to take, but a structured way to evaluate whether something is appropriate for your specific medication context. The goal is to reduce guessing, not add to it.
items.question: I'm scared to try anything new after gabapentin or HRT made things worse. How is this different?

items.answer: Most herb guides are organized by plant and written for people who aren't on medications. This handbook is organized by situation and built around triage logic: your symptoms first, then a safety screen, then conservative options with clear flags. The Quick-Scan Tables are built into every entry — not buried in footnotes.
items.question: I've tried herb guides before and they didn't address my situation. How is this different?

items.answer: No. The Honest Herbalist Handbook is an educational reference only. It is not intended to diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent any condition. The Med/HRT Conversation Pack is specifically designed to help you have a better conversation with your healthcare provider — not to replace that conversation.
items.question: Does this replace my doctor?

items.answer: You shouldn't have to take our word for it. The handbook includes evidence grading for every remedy — clearly labeling what is research-supported versus what is traditional use only. The Quick-Scan flag system is based on known drug class interactions, not marketing claims. And if it doesn't deliver on that promise, you have a full refund within 30 days.
items.question: I don't trust 'safe' claims — everyone contradicts each other online. Why should I trust this?

items.answer: The handbook is a digital download in PDF format, accessible immediately after purchase on any device. The Quick-Scan Sheets, Conversation Pack, Interaction Tripwires Checklist, and Tracker are all printable if you prefer a physical reference.
items.question: What format is the handbook?

items.answer: Email us within 30 days for a full refund. No questions designed to talk you out of it. The 30-day no-guessing guarantee is unconditional.
items.question: What if I'm not satisfied?

items.answer: No. The Honest Herbalist Handbook is an educational reference only. It is not intended to diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent any condition. It is designed to support — not replace — the guidance of your healthcare provider.
